column1: What are the two main internal components of the CPU?
column2: The Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) and the Control Unit (CU), plus a set of registers.

column1: What does the Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) do?
column2: Carries out all arithmetic operations (add, subtract, etc.), logical operations (AND, OR, NOT, comparisons) and shifts; the result is normally placed in the Accumulator.

column1: What does the Control Unit (CU) do?
column2: Decodes each instruction, generates control signals to direct other components, manages data flow along the buses, and synchronises operations using the system clock.

column1: Define a register.
column2: A small, extremely fast block of memory inside the CPU used to temporarily hold data during processing.

column1: What is the role of the Program Counter (PC)?
column2: Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched; it is incremented during each cycle.

column1: What is the role of the Memory Address Register (MAR)?
column2: Holds the address of the memory location currently being read from or written to.

column1: What is the role of the Memory Data Register (MDR)?
column2: Holds the data or instruction just fetched from, or about to be written to, memory.

column1: What is the role of the Current Instruction Register (CIR)?
column2: Holds the current instruction while it is being decoded and executed.

column1: What is the role of the Accumulator (ACC)?
column2: Stores the immediate results of calculations carried out by the ALU.

column1: What does the address bus carry and in which direction?
column2: Carries the memory address of the location to read/write; unidirectional (CPU to memory).

column1: What does the data bus carry and in which direction?
column2: Carries the actual data/instructions transferred between CPU and memory; bidirectional.

column1: What does the control bus carry and in which direction?
column2: Carries control signals (e.g. read/write, clock, interrupt request); bidirectional.

column1: List the steps of the FETCH phase of the FDE cycle.
column2: 1) Address in PC copied to MAR; 2) instruction fetched along data bus into MDR (read signal on control bus); 3) PC incremented; 4) contents of MDR copied into CIR.

column1: What happens in the DECODE phase of the FDE cycle?
column2: The Control Unit decodes the instruction in the CIR, splitting it into the opcode (the operation) and the operand (the data/address it acts on).

column1: What happens in the EXECUTE phase of the FDE cycle?
column2: The decoded instruction is carried out, e.g. the ALU performs a calculation (result to ACC), data is loaded/stored, or the PC is changed (a jump); the cycle then repeats.

column1: How is a jump/branch instruction carried out in machine code?
column2: A new address is written directly into the PC during the execute phase, so the next fetch comes from a different location (this implements loops and selection).

column1: What is clock speed and how is it measured?
column2: The rate at which the clock generates pulses to synchronise CPU operations, measured in hertz (Hz); e.g. 3.5 GHz = 3.5 billion cycles per second.

column1: How does a higher clock speed affect performance, and what limits it?
column2: More FDE cycles per second means faster processing; limited by rising heat dissipation and power consumption.

column1: What is a core?
column2: An independent processing unit within a CPU capable of running its own fetch-decode-execute cycle.

column1: Why does adding more cores not always increase performance?
column2: Only helps if the software is written to use multiple cores and the task can be split; data dependencies, sequential work and coordination overheads limit gains.

column1: What is cache and why does it improve performance?
column2: A small amount of very fast memory on or near the CPU that stores frequently/recently used instructions and data, so the CPU avoids slower fetches from main memory (RAM).

column1: Compare L1, L2 and L3 cache.
column2: L1 is smallest and fastest, on the core; L2 is larger but slower; L3 is largest and slowest of the three (still faster than RAM) and is often shared between cores.

column1: What is pipelining?
column2: Splitting instruction processing into stages so that while one instruction is executed, the next is decoded and a third is fetched simultaneously, increasing throughput.

column1: What is a branch hazard in pipelining?
column2: When a taken jump/branch invalidates instructions already fetched ahead of it, so they must be flushed/discarded; mitigated by branch prediction.

column1: Describe Von Neumann architecture.
column2: A single shared memory and bus holds both program instructions and data, accessed over a shared bus.

column1: Describe Harvard architecture.
column2: Instructions and data are stored in physically separate memories with separate buses, allowing them to be accessed at the same time.

column1: What is the Von Neumann bottleneck?
column2: Because data and instructions share one memory and bus, an instruction cannot be fetched while data is read/written at the same time, limiting throughput.

column1: Give a typical use of Von Neumann and of Harvard architecture.
column2: Von Neumann: general-purpose computers (PCs, laptops). Harvard: embedded systems, microcontrollers and DSPs.

column1: Describe CISC processors.
column2: Complex Instruction Set Computer: a large set of complex, variable-length instructions; complexity is in hardware; instructions may take several cycles; e.g. desktop x86.

column1: Describe RISC processors.
column2: Reduced Instruction Set Computer: a small set of simple, fixed-length instructions aiming for one per cycle; complexity in software/compiler; easier to pipeline; e.g. ARM.

column1: Why does RISC suit mobile/battery devices?
column2: Simple, uniform, single-cycle instructions make pipelining and parallelism easier and reduce power consumption, ideal for smartphones and tablets.

column1: What is a GPU?
column2: A processor with thousands of small cores specialised for performing the same operation on large blocks of data in parallel (highly parallel floating-point work).

column1: Give two non-graphics uses of a GPU.
column2: Machine learning / training neural networks, scientific/numerical simulations, image/video processing, or cryptocurrency mining/brute-force hashing.

column1: What kind of work are GPUs poor at compared with CPUs?
column2: Heavily sequential, branch-heavy logic with data dependencies; that work suits the few powerful high-clock cores of a CPU.

column1: Define parallel processing.
column2: Carrying out multiple computations simultaneously, splitting a task across several processors/cores to complete it faster.

column1: Why do some tasks not parallelise well?
column2: Data dependencies (a step needs a previous result), splitting/coordination/recombination overheads, and some algorithms being fundamentally sequential.

column1: How does magnetic storage (HDD/tape) store data?
column2: Data is stored as magnetised patterns (polarity) on a spinning platter or tape; a read/write head detects or changes the magnetisation.

column1: Give one pro and one con of magnetic storage.
column2: Pro: very large capacity at low cost per GB. Con: slower than flash and fragile due to moving parts.

column1: How does flash/solid-state storage store data?
column2: A type of EEPROM that stores data by trapping electrons in transistors (memory cells), with no moving parts (e.g. SSD, USB stick, SD card).

column1: Give one pro and one con of flash storage.
column2: Pro: very fast, silent, low power, durable and shock-resistant. Con: more expensive per GB and has a finite number of write cycles.

column1: How does optical storage work?
column2: A laser reads/writes data by detecting pits and lands on a reflective surface, sensing changes in reflected light (e.g. CD, DVD, Blu-ray).

column1: Compare RAM and ROM.
column2: RAM is volatile, read and write, holds programs/data currently in use. ROM is non-volatile, normally read only, holds permanent boot/firmware (bootstrap) instructions.

column1: What is virtual memory?
column2: When RAM is full, the OS uses a portion of secondary storage (a swap/paging file) as if it were extra RAM, moving pages in and out as needed.

column1: Scenario: a travelling laptop needs internal storage. Which storage type and why?
column2: Flash/SSD: no moving parts so shock-resistant and durable, fast, silent, low power for better battery life, and lightweight/portable.

column1: Scenario: cheap long-term archiving of huge data volumes. Which storage type and why?
column2: Magnetic (HDD or tape): very large capacity at low cost per GB; tape is cheap and reliable for archive despite slow serial access.
